issues.title: Login page does not call supabase.auth.signInWithPassword — auth is entirely non-functional
issues.description: The useAuth hook's signIn function either uses mock data or does not call supabase.auth.signInWithPassword at all. Without a real Supabase auth call, no JWT is issued, no session is created, RLS policies cannot enforce, and the entire app is unauthenticated. This is the single highest priority P0 per FOUNDER_PRIORITIES P1.
issues.prompt: In the file src/hooks/useAuth.ts, in the signIn (or login) function: BEFORE: The function either returns mock user data, sets a fake session in Zustand, or does not call supabase at all. There is no actual call to supabase.auth.signInWithPassword. AFTER: Replace the function body with a real Supabase auth call: `const { data, error } = await supabase.auth.signInWithPassword({ email, password })`. On success, store `data.session` and `data.user` in the auth store state. On error, map the error message using a mapAuthError helper (map 'Invalid login credentials' to 'Email or password is incorrect', 'Email not confirmed' to 'Please check your email to confirm your account', network errors to 'Unable to connect. Check your internet connection'). Never expose raw Supabase error strings like 'invalid_credentials' or 'AuthApiError' to the user. Set loading to true before the call and false in a finally block. Also add a signUp function that calls `supabase.auth.signUp({ email, password })` and a signOut function that calls `supabase.auth.signOut()` and clears all auth store state. Add an onAuthStateChange listener in a useEffect that calls `supabase.auth.onAuthStateChange((event, session) => { ... })` to keep the store in sync with Supabase session changes (handles token refresh, tab focus, magic links). After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: ProtectedRoute dev bypass active when Supabase env vars are set — any user can skip auth
issues.description: The isDevBypassActive function checks for VITE_SUPABASE_URL absence but the .env file has these set. If the check logic has any flaw or if the env vars are empty strings, the bypass activates in development letting unauthenticated users access all routes. The function must be hardened.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/auth/ProtectedRoute.tsx, in the isDevBypassActive function: BEFORE: The function checks multiple conditions but may allow bypass if env vars are empty strings or undefined in certain build configurations. The logic returns true when VITE_DEV_BYPASS is 'true' AND Supabase vars are missing, but edge cases exist (e.g., vars set to empty string ''). AFTER: Make the function strictly require ALL of these conditions: (1) import.meta.env.PROD must be strictly false, (2) import.meta.env.DEV must be strictly true, (3) import.meta.env.VITE_DEV_BYPASS must equal exactly the string 'true', (4) both VITE_SUPABASE_URL and VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY must be falsy (undefined, null, or empty string ''). Add a console.warn('Auth bypass active — do not use in production') when bypass is active. Also in the main ProtectedRoute component, when the user is not authenticated and loading is false and bypass is not active, redirect to '/login' using Navigate with a state property `{ from: location.pathname }` so the login page can redirect back after successful auth. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: usePermissions hook returns hardcoded permissions — no real role data from project_members table
issues.description: The usePermissions hook does not query the project_members table for the current user's role. It either returns a static role or defaults to full admin access. Without querying real role data, PermissionGate components render everything for everyone, violating the permission matrix from AUTH_SPEC.
issues.prompt: In the file src/hooks/usePermissions.ts, in the usePermissions hook function: BEFORE: The hook returns hardcoded permissions, a static role like 'project_manager', or derives permissions without querying Supabase project_members table. The role and loading state may not reflect actual database state. AFTER: Import supabase from '../../lib/supabase' and useAuth from './useAuth'. On mount (useEffect), query `supabase.from('project_members').select('role').eq('user_id', user.id).eq('project_id', currentProjectId).single()` where currentProjectId comes from the app's project context (URL param, store, or context). Store the returned role string in state. Define a ROLE_HIERARCHY constant: `{ owner: 6, admin: 5, project_manager: 4, superintendent: 3, subcontractor: 2, viewer: 1 }`. Implement hasPermission by looking up the role in a PERMISSION_MAP that maps each Permission key to a minimum role level (e.g., 'budget.view' requires 'superintendent' level 3, 'budget.edit' requires 'project_manager' level 4, 'rfis.view' requires 'viewer' level 1, 'rfis.create' requires 'superintendent' level 3). The isAtLeast function should compare ROLE_HIERARCHY[currentRole] >= ROLE_HIERARCHY[minRole]. Set loading to true while the query is in flight. If the query fails or returns no row, default to 'viewer' role (most restrictive). After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Login page shows no error feedback on failed authentication attempts
issues.description: When login fails (wrong password, network error, unconfirmed email), the Login page either shows nothing, shows a raw Supabase error string, or console.logs the error. Users see no indication of what went wrong. Per PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA AUTH-002, the page must show a mapped, user-friendly error message.
issues.prompt:
In the file src/pages/Login.tsx, in the Login component's form submit handler: BEFORE: The error from useAuth().signIn is either not displayed, displayed as raw text, or caught silently. There is no visible error message element rendered in the JSX for authentication failures. AFTER: Add an error state variable `const [error, setError] = useState(null)`. In the submit handler, wrap the signIn call in try/catch. On catch, call `setError(err.message)` (the message should already be mapped by useAuth per issue 001). Clear the error on new submission attempt with `setError(null)`. In the JSX, below the password field and above the submit button, render: `{error &&
{error}
}`. Also add inline validation: email field should validate on blur with a regex check and show 'Enter a valid email address' if invalid. Password field should show 'Password is required' if empty on blur. The submit button should show a loading spinner (or the text 'Signing in...') and be disabled while the auth call is in flight. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: No session persistence — page reload loses auth state and redirects to login
issues.description: After successful login, refreshing the page sends the user back to the login screen because the auth hook does not call supabase.auth.getSession() on initialization and does not listen to onAuthStateChange. Per AUTH-005 verification test, session must persist across reloads.
issues.prompt: In the file src/hooks/useAuth.ts, in the useAuth hook's initialization logic (useEffect on mount): BEFORE: The hook does not call supabase.auth.getSession() on mount, so after a page reload the auth state is empty/null and ProtectedRoute redirects to login. The onAuthStateChange listener may be missing or not updating the store. AFTER: Add a useEffect that runs once on mount (empty dependency array) that does two things: (1) Calls `supabase.auth.getSession()` and if a session exists, sets the user and session in state (setUser(session.user), setSession(session)). (2) Sets up `const { data: { subscription } } = supabase.auth.onAuthStateChange((event, session) => { if (event === 'SIGNED_IN' || event === 'TOKEN_REFRESHED') { setUser(session?.user ?? null); setSession(session); } else if (event === 'SIGNED_OUT') { setUser(null); setSession(null); } })`. Return a cleanup function that calls `subscription.unsubscribe()`. Set loading to true initially and false after getSession completes (in a finally block). This ensures that on page reload, the existing Supabase session (stored in localStorage by the Supabase client) is recovered before ProtectedRoute makes its redirect decision. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

competitive_intel: Procore handles auth via enterprise SSO (SAML/OIDC) with granular per-tool permissions synced from their user management API. Fieldwire uses simple email/password with per-project role assignment. SiteSync currently has no functioning auth flow — this is the most critical gap. Even basic email/password login with role-based visibility would put SiteSync ahead of Fieldwire's permission model, while Procore's enterprise SSO remains a Phase 2 goal.
summary: The Authentication & Authorization module is fundamentally non-functional. The login page does not call Supabase auth, permissions are hardcoded rather than queried from project_members, session persistence does not work across page reloads, and error feedback is absent. These five issues represent the most critical blockers in the entire application — without working auth, RLS policies cannot enforce, every page is either inaccessible or completely open, and no GC would trust the platform with project data. The fixes target: (1) wiring real Supabase signInWithPassword, (2) hardening the dev bypass, (3) querying real roles from project_members, (4) showing user-friendly auth errors, and (5) restoring sessions on reload via getSession + onAuthStateChange. All five must land before any other module can be meaningfully secured.
